title: How Software Engineers Can Survive AI
author: Antoine Azar
text:
How Software Engineers Can Survive AI
Software engineering is about to go through a rough ride — but a specific type of engineers will thrive in the post-AI world.
I love coding. I wrote my first line of code at age 6. Decades of experience later and today as engineering director at Brex, I spend a lot of time thinking about the future of our profession.
You likely read Mark Zuckerberg's quote last week: “AI will replace mid-level software engineers in 2025.”
Will the software engineering profession die?
Are we all turning into commodities?
The bad news, and the hard truth we need to accept: our party is ending.
It's hard to complain — software did this to countless industries: streaming ruined the party for record labels. Netflix ruined the party for movie theatres. Uber ruined the party for taxis.
The kinda good news: those industries didn’t die. They shrunk and mutated (Movie theatres may have just shrunk. Taxis have at least improved a bit. Record labels shrunk, mutated, and grew again).
AI will ruin the party for SWEs, but it won’t kill us.
Our industry will likely shrink. We won't need the hordes of software developers the world consumes today. "Sweatshops" will disappear. As a plus, we'll get rid of the term "code monkey".
As Darwin laid out, those who can adapt will thrive. It will be pure survival of the fittest in the next decade. Those able to mutate will survive, and turn into the dominant species.
As software engineers, this is a critical time to rethink the value we bring to the table. Here’s how we can future-proof our careers and leverage AI for greater benefit.
The Post-AI Engineer
If AI can code, post-AI engineers need to think beyond the code. They need to embrace a broader perspective, and connect technical expertise with insights from separate domains to drive impactful outcomes.
I-shaped (aka uni-dimensional) engineers are at risk of extinction, with the exception of researchers and a handful of deep tech developers.
For most of the industry, T-shaped engineers, combining technical depth with cross-functional breadth, will be the bare minimum.
The thriving post-AI engineer will be multi-spired, spiking in multiple domains like product management, design, marketing, sales, or business strategy, with spires of varying heights, and a foundation of soft skills.
The ability to blend excellence across disciplines is what drives innovation and outsized value.
This notion has been proven time and again.
Steve Jobs created the world’s most valuable company by bridging the world's most advanced technology with excellent design and incredible marketing. At the root, if Jobs had not taken a typography class in college, and combined it with tech and business, Apple might have never existed.
Brex's meteoric rise wasn’t just due to great engineering; it was the result of a keen understanding of customer needs (product design), a sharp focus on scaling intelligently (business strategy), and multiple innovations such as a new underwriting model, frictionless credit and programmable corporate cards deeply integrated in a spend management platform (domain expertise).
Engineers who can cross dimensions are indispensable because they can bridge the gap between what users want and what the technology can deliver.
AI Can't Compete With a Multi-Spired Engineer
AI today (and likely for the next few years) has two weak points working to human engineers' advantage:
First, AI thrives in narrow, well-defined contexts. Optimize a database query? AI can do that. Generate boilerplate code? AI’s got it covered. But ask AI to generate new insights by combining disparate domains, and it falters badly (as an example, just try asking ChatGPT for good startup ideas).
Second, and more importantly, AI is by definition of its training sets a mediocre average of what humanity has already produced. Value, on the other hand, is created by inventing something humanity has not seen before.
Until AI develops the ability to extrapolate innovation and develop customer empathy, those skills are safe.
Eight Steps to Future-Proof Your Career
Here’s how you can start building your multi-spired skill set:
1. Deepen Your Technical Expertise
Even in a world dominated by AI, technical depth matters. AI tools like GitHub Copilot or ChatGPT are only as good as the engineers guiding them. Understanding system design, scalable architecture, and even niche domains like blockchain or quantum computing can set you apart. Elevate your main spire.
2. Learn Product Management
Great products are built at the intersection of technology, user needs, and business goals. Learning how to prioritize features, manage roadmaps, and empathize with users will make you an invaluable asset to any team. Elevating your thinking to deeply understand business outcomes will make you invaluable to your leadership.
3. Explore Design Thinking
Design isn’t just about making things look pretty; it’s about solving problems creatively. Learn the basics of UX/UI design, user research, and prototyping. A software engineer who can also sketch wireframes and talk to customers is a rare and valuable hybrid.
4. Understand Marketing and Sales
In the startup world, engineers often dismiss marketing and sales as “not my job.” But understanding how products are sold, distributed, and monetized can give you a significant edge. Engineers who can translate technical jargon into customer value are indispensable.
5. Develop Business Acumen
Familiarize yourself with key business concepts like unit economics, pricing strategies, and competitive analysis. The ability to understand the “why” behind technical decisions can position you as a leader, not just a contributor.
6. Build up Soft and Social Skills
Soft skills will help you build your persuasion power and influence. Being a better communicator is in large part an acquired skill. Ask for coaching and mentoring from colleagues who excel in this domain, and read some of the foundational literature in the domain (e.g. a great start is How to Win Friends and Influence People by Dale Carnegie).
7. Learn How to Use Various AI Tools
Getting a head start in the domain will put you at an advantage compared to most of your peers. You'll get a deep understanding of where AI excels and where it is lacking, allowing you to wield it effectively while developing your skills in the areas it can't compete in.
8. Practice at Work
Choose to work at a company that values the development of adjacent skillsets, and offers you the opportunity to learn, grow and practice your new skills. Don't waste professional years being pigeon-holed in a specialization with fast depreciation. Hint: startups are a great place for this, provided a solid team and good mentors.
The Road Ahead
The future of our profession doesn't compete with AI, it complements it. By developing expertise across multiple domains, we become more than today's engineers. We turn to creators, strategists, and innovators.
In a world where AI might write the code, the most valuable engineers will be the ones who decide what to build and why. By embracing the spire-shaped model, you can ensure that your career doesn’t just survive — it thrives.
Don't delay, start today. Don't wait for layoffs to hit the industry to be ready. Be curious, learn a new skill, shadow a different team, or tackle a problem outside your comfort zone. The future belongs to those who will elevate multiple spires to the sky.
